critic_raw:
Issue 1 — Offline Precision Does Not Measure Spread Reduction
The evaluation compares the classifier to a keyword filter on a held-out set of labeled posts. This measures whether the classifier is better at identifying policy-violating content. It does not measure whether deploying the classifier reduces the reach of misinformation. Reducing spread requires that the detection happens early in a post's virality trajectory and that subsequent removal or down-ranking actually limits sharing. Neither of these is in scope of the offline evaluation.

Issue 2 — Effect Pathway Depends on Moderator Workflow and Platform Architecture
Even assuming perfect detection, spread reduction is contingent on: how quickly reviewers act on flags, whether high-reach posts (which drive most spread) are prioritized in the review queue, whether the classifier covers private and closed-group content where spread may concentrate, and whether users adapt to evasive phrasing. The report explicitly does not compare high-reach accounts with ordinary users or analyze whether the model focuses review capacity on high-spread content. This workflow dependence means the effect of deployment is unknown even if offline metrics are strong.
defender_raw:
Pass 1 — Analysis (no critic visible):
Issue 1: The gap between offline classification metrics and real-world spread reduction is the fundamental problem. The report's conclusion says the classifier 'reduces misinformation spread' — this is a behavioral outcome claim that requires evidence about content reach, not just detection accuracy. Concede.
Issue 2: The workflow pathway to spread reduction is entirely unanalyzed. Removal latency, queue prioritization, coverage of private groups — all missing. Concede.

Pass 2 — Verdicts: Both conceded. Verdict: critique_wins.
adjudication_raw:
ADJUDICATION — scope_intent_moderation_308 — Isolated Debate Run 2

Consistent with Run 1. Both issues conceded independently.

Verdict: CRITIQUE_WINS
